summary: The main claim is: Since that system is better at AI design than its human creators, it should be able to design an AI better than itself.
The main claim is: This is why we need more public debates on AI development, as AI will fundamentally transform our world, for better or worse.
As AI risk gets into the mainstream, the conversation should become much broader.
The main claim is: add in a steering vector to either the first residual stream, or to all residual streams.
We run each sentence through GPT-2, with and without the "weddings" steering vector.

top_comment.author: JenniferRM
top_comment.text:
I was educated by this, and surprised, and appreciate the whole thing! This part jumped out at me because it seemed like something people trying to "show off, but not really explain" would have not bothered to write about (and also I had an idea):

[...]

The thought I had was maybe to describe the desired behavior, and explain a plausible cause in terms of well known kinds of mental configurations that speakers can be in, and also demonstrate it directly? (Plus a countervailing description, demonstration, and distinct causal theory.)

So perhaps a steering vector made from these phrases could work: "I'm from Quebec et je glisse souvent accidentellement vers le français" - "I only speak English because I'm a monolingual American".

EDIT: If you have the tooling set up to swiftly try this experiment, maybe it helps to explain the most central theory that motivates it, and might gain bayes points if it works?

According to the "LLMs are Textual Soul Engines" hypothesis, most of the 1600 dimensions are related to ways that "generative" sources of text (authors, characters, reasons-for-talking, etc) could relate to things (words (and "that which nouns and verbs and grammar refer to in general")).

The above steering vector (if the hypothesis applies here) would/should basically inject a "persona vector" into the larger operations of a sort of "soul engine".

The prompts I'm suggesting, by construction, explicitly should(?) produce a persona that tends to switch from English to French (and be loyal to Quebec (and have other "half-random latent/stereotypical features")).

I'm...

summary: The main claim is: we are having our AIs spend 10% of their time on alignment then it is 10% of what the AI is optimized to do.” I think there is a big scheduling question here: which tasks first?

top_comment.author: Raemon
top_comment.text: Quick admin note: by default, lines that are bold create a Table of Contents heading (which resulted in the ToC having a whole bunch of spurious [Christiano] and [GA] lines). There's a cute hack to get around this, by inserting a space in italics at the end of the bolded line. I just used my admin powers to add the space-with-italics to all the "[Christiano]", "[GA]", etc, so that the ToC is more readable.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: Quick admin note: by default, lines that are bold create a Table of Contents heading (which resulted in the ToC having a whole bunch of spurious [Christiano] and [GA] lines).

summary: The main claim is: I do not claim that research towards building the independent AI thinkers of 2.1 above is the most effective AI alignment research intervention, nor that it is the most effective intervention for moral progress.
The main claim is: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback (2022).
Legal standards can be learned directly from legal data with LLMs.
The main claim is: the gist of it is: the goal of the AI should be to assist the user by picking policies which maximize the user's utility function .
in a given hypothesis, the AI is simply described as a part of the world, same as any other part.
The main claim is: If a simulation is powerful enough to be undetectable for humans, then it should be at least powerful enough to be undetectable for an AI.
Contrariwise, if the best simulation is poor enough that an AI can detect it, then it should be at least poor enough for humans to detect it, as well.
The main claim is: Why do I call Alex’s training strategy “baseline” HFDT?
They then train Alex on the new training data, and/or with the new reward signal.
The main claim is: If we want more people in the world who understand AGI safety, we need a place where it's accepted and encouraged to ask about the basics.
This is a safe space for people to ask stupid questions, so be kind!

The main claim is: I’m still trying to think through what I expect to happen if alignment turns out to be hard, and this more like the most recent entry in a long journey of gradually-improving stories.

In Buck's transcript for his talk on Cruxes for working on AI safety there's an example of a bad argument for why we should be worried about people building spaceships too small.
